example_english: This movie is amazing!
origin: Internet slang from 2010, popularized by a dubbed Japanese anime; now mainstream

example_english: Today's weather is really disappointing.
origin: Negation of 给力; equally common in everyday speech

example_english: You're amazing!
origin: Colloquial abbreviation of 牛逼 (niúbī); the short form is socially safe

example_english: This person is pretty reliable.
origin: Northern dialect, now standard colloquial nationwide

example_english: What he said is unreliable.
origin: Negation of 靠谱; very common in daily speech

example_english: She's very attractive (has a high looks score).
origin: Internet coinage combining 颜 (face) + 值 (value); ubiquitous since ~2015

example_english: He's the top student in our class.
origin: Campus slang; 霸 (tyrant/dominator) applied to academics

example_english: I'm just a terrible student.
origin: Opposite of 学霸; self-deprecating campus humor

example_sentence: 我是一个吃货，什么都想尝尝。
example_english: I'm a foodie — I want to try everything.
origin: Originally mildly pejorative, now a proud self-label

example_english: He lives a go-with-the-flow lifestyle.
origin: From Japanese Buddhist-style (仏系); viral in China from 2017

example_english: Young people say they want to just lie flat.
origin: 2021 social movement term rejecting overwork culture (996); now mainstream

example_english: Education today is way too competitive.
origin: Academic term (involution) adopted as social commentary ~2020

example_english: This song is the GOAT!
origin: Pinyin abbreviation of 永远的神 (eternal god); gaming origin, now general praise

meaning: check in/clock in/visit a trendy spot
example_sentence: 我们去那家网红店打卡吧。
example_english: Let's go check in at that influencer restaurant.
origin: Originally meant clocking in at work; now means visiting trendy places for social media

example_english: This is an influencer-famous restaurant.
origin: 网 (internet) + 红 (popular/red); also used as adjective for viral things

meaning: to plant desire/to make someone want something
example_sentence: 她给我种草了一款口红。
example_english: She made me want to buy a lipstick.
origin: E-commerce/influencer culture metaphor: planting desire like grass

meaning: to finally buy something/satisfy a want
example_english: I finally bought those shoes I'd been wanting.
origin: Counterpart to 种草; pulling the grass of desire by purchasing

example_sentence: 他是老司机了，跟着他就行。
example_english: He's a veteran — just follow his lead.
origin: Literally 'old driver'; internet slang for someone who knows the ropes

example_english: That actor is a young heartthrob.
origin: Literally 'little fresh meat'; entertainment industry term for young male idols

example_english: I went on another shopping spree on Singles' Day.
origin: Literally 'chop hands' — I should cut off my hands to stop buying

example_english: This gift is really thoughtful.
origin: Literally 'walk through the heart'; opposite is 走形式 (going through motions)

example_english: That remark really hits where it hurts.
origin: Literally 'pierce the heart'; from 2017 meme 扎心了老铁

meaning: you've worked hard/thanks for your effort
example_english: Everyone, thanks for your hard work!
origin: Standard polite expression in workplaces; less formal than written thanks

example_english: What you said makes total sense.
origin: Northern colloquial; 毛病 = fault/problem; used as agreement/approval

example_english: Don't play tricks on me.
origin: Originally a martial arts term for fixed routines; now means manipulation or formulaic approach

example_english: This leader is very down-to-earth.
origin: Literally 'connected to earth energy'; used for politicians, products, and people

example_english: This restaurant is very upscale.
origin: Abbreviation of 高端大气上档次 (high-end, grand, and classy); often slightly ironic

example_english: Exam tomorrow — you can do it!
origin: Literally 'add fuel/oil'; universally used encouragement across all contexts

meaning: to drop the ball/fail at a critical moment
example_english: Don't drop the ball at the critical moment.
origin: Literally 'drop the chain' (bicycle chain); means to fail when it matters most

example_sentence: 你这个想法真是脑洞大开。
example_english: Your idea is wildly creative.
origin: Literally 'brain hole wide open'; internet term for creative/absurd thinking

example_sentence: 他总是吐槽公司的食堂。
example_english: He always roasts the company cafeteria.
origin: Borrowed from Japanese ツッコミ (tsukkomi) comedy tradition; mainstream since ~2010

example_english: He's great at telling jokes.
origin: Originally from traditional comedy (相声); now means any funny story or internet joke

meaning: to take over a bad deal/inherit someone's mess
example_english: Don't make me take over that mess.
origin: Stock market term (catch a falling stock); now used for any undesirable inheritance

example_sentence: 我有点社恐，不太喜欢聚会。
example_english: I have social anxiety — I don't really like parties.
origin: Abbreviation of 社交恐惧症; widely adopted self-label among young Chinese

example_sentence: 她是个社牛，到哪都能交朋友。
example_english: She's a total social butterfly — she makes friends everywhere.
origin: Opposite of 社恐; 牛 = awesome; a person who excels at socializing

meaning: to slack off/goof off at work
example_english: Everyone was slacking off in the afternoon.
origin: From idiom 浑水摸鱼 (fish in muddy waters); now means killing time at work

meaning: inspirational platitudes/chicken soup for the soul
example_english: Stop feeding me inspirational nonsense.
origin: From 'Chicken Soup for the Soul'; often used critically for empty motivation

example_english: Here's a dose of cynical reality for today.
origin: Poison + chicken soup = darkly humorous counter-motivational sayings

example_english: This article is packed with useful information.
origin: Literally 'dry goods'; internet content term for substantive, no-filler material

example_english: I just coasted through work today.
origin: Swimming metaphor: paddling gently on the surface instead of swimming hard

example_english: Today's game — let's go!
origin: Popularized by video blogger; enthusiastic exclamation of encouragement

meaning: humble-bragging/showing off while pretending to complain
example_sentence: 她说减肥太难了只瘦了十斤，真凡尔赛。
example_english: She says dieting is so hard, she only lost 10 jin — what a humble-brag.
origin: Named after Versailles; from a 2020 manga about aristocratic lifestyle; now means humble-bragging

meaning: face-slapping reversal/I take it back, this is great
example_sentence: 之前说不吃，现在真香了吧？
example_english: You said you wouldn't eat it — face-slapping reversal, huh?
origin: From a reality TV show where a participant refused food then ate enthusiastically, saying 真香 (really fragrant)

example_english: Bro, what do you think?
origin: Northeast dialect 铁哥们 (iron buddy); popularized on livestream platforms

example_english: Once you hear this song, you get hooked.
origin: Originally described alcohol going to your head; now used for any intense emotional hook

example_sentence: 他讲的量子力学我虽然不明觉厉，但还是鼓了掌。
example_english: His quantum mechanics talk — I didn't understand but it sounded impressive, so I clapped anyway.
origin: Internet abbreviation of 虽然不明白，但觉得很厉害; used to admit ignorance while giving credit

meaning: life is hard enough, don't expose the truth
example_sentence: 他以为自己唱得好，人艰不拆吧。
example_english: He thinks he sings well — life's hard enough, let him have it.
origin: Abbreviation of 人生已经如此艰难，有些事就不要拆穿了; a plea for merciful silence

meaning: the more you think about it, the scarier it gets
example_english: This news story gets scarier the more you think about it.
origin: Four-character internet coinage following chengyu form; used for creepy realizations

example_english: This person thinks too small.
origin: Originally 'pattern/layout'; now a trendy term for someone's strategic vision or lack thereof

example_english: Technology empowers traditional industries.
origin: Corporate buzzword borrowed from 'empower'; used heavily in tech/startup culture

example_english: We need to benchmark against industry leaders.
origin: Business jargon from 'benchmarking'; now also used casually for comparison

example_english: We need to close the loop on this.
origin: Engineering term adopted into corporate speak; means completing a full cycle with no loose ends

meaning: to nail it/have perfect control of
example_sentence: 这个角色被她拿捏得死死的。
example_english: She absolutely nailed this role.
origin: Originally 'to grasp/handle'; internet usage emphasizes masterful control or perfect execution

example_sentence: 同事们都在卷，我压力好大。
example_english: All my colleagues are grinding hard — I'm under so much pressure.
origin: Shortened from 内卷; used as a verb meaning to engage in relentless competition

example_english: Her style has that effortlessly cool vibe.
origin: 2023-era aesthetic term praising a relaxed, unstressed demeanor as aspirational

meaning: someone who says what you're thinking/spokesperson for your feelings
example_english: This blogger says exactly what I'm thinking.
origin: 嘴 (mouth) + 替 (substitute); internet term for someone who articulates your unspoken thoughts

example_sentence: 听完他的话，我如醍醐灌顶。
example_english: After hearing his words, I had a sudden epiphany.
origin: Buddhist metaphor: clarified butter (醍醐) poured on the head; classic chengyu used in modern educated speech

meaning: to strategize from behind the scenes/mastermind
example_sentence: 他运筹帷幄，一切尽在掌控。
example_english: He masterminds everything from behind the scenes — all under control.
origin: From Sima Qian's Records: 运筹帷幄之中，决胜千里之外; describes strategic genius

meaning: to crush the competition from a higher level/dimensional reduction attack
example_sentence: 苹果进入新市场就是降维打击。
example_english: Apple entering a new market is basically a dimensional-reduction attack.
origin: From Liu Cixin's Three-Body Problem (三体); now means overwhelming superiority from a higher plane

meaning: to break through one's emotional defenses/to be deeply moved
example_sentence: 看到最后那一幕，我彻底破防了。
example_english: Seeing that final scene, my emotional defenses completely crumbled.
origin: Gaming term (break defense); now means being emotionally overwhelmed despite trying to stay composed

meaning: the ceiling/the ultimate/the GOAT in a category
example_english: She is the absolute ceiling of acting skill.
origin: Literally 'ceiling'; internet usage means the highest possible level — always used as a compliment

example_sentence: 听到上课铃声就紧张，刻在DNA里了。
example_english: Getting anxious at a school bell — it's hardwired into my DNA.
origin: Internet hyperbole for deeply conditioned responses or cultural instincts

meaning: far ahead/way out in front (often ironic/meme)
example_english: Our technology is far ahead.
origin: Standard phrase meaning 'far in the lead'; became a meme from Huawei launch events, now used both sincerely and ironically

example_sentence: 别精神内耗了，先行动起来。
example_english: Stop overthinking and draining yourself — just take action.
origin: Psychology-adjacent term; describes exhaustion from internal anxiety, self-doubt, and overthinking without acting

example_sentence: 我是i人，社交对我来说很累。
example_english: I'm an introvert — socializing exhausts me.
origin: From MBTI personality types; i = introvert, e = extrovert; extremely common youth self-identification

example_english: I'm looking for a meal buddy.
origin: Originally a Shanghai dialect word for card game partner; now means any activity-specific companion (饭搭子, 旅游搭子, etc.)

meaning: too refined to be popular/highbrow and lonely
example_sentence: 他的作品曲高和寡，知音难觅。
example_english: His work is too refined for mass appeal — kindred spirits are hard to find.
origin: From Song Yu's 'Answer to the King of Chu'; describes art or ideas too elevated for common understanding

meaning: seeing only a small part of the whole/limited perspective
example_sentence: 这不过是管中窥豹，可见一斑。
example_english: This is just a glimpse through a tube — but you can see the pattern.
origin: From Shishuo Xinyu; seeing a leopard through a bamboo tube — partial but suggestive understanding

meaning: to prepare for a rainy day/take precautions
example_sentence: 企业要未雨绸缪，提前做好风险管理。
example_english: Enterprises should prepare for a rainy day and manage risks in advance.
origin: From the Book of Songs; literally 'before it rains, bind the window frames'; used in formal/business contexts

meaning: to align on the level of detail/granularity (corporate jargon)
example_sentence: 我们先对齐一下颗粒度。
example_english: Let's first align on the level of granularity.
origin: Peak corporate buzzword combining 对齐 (align) + 颗粒度 (granularity); widely mocked as hollow jargon

example_sentence: 政府打出了一套政策组合拳。
example_english: The government rolled out a combination punch of policies.
origin: Boxing metaphor for a coordinated series of actions; heavily used in policy and business reporting

example_sentence: 扶贫政策要精准滴灌，不能大水漫灌。
example_english: Poverty relief must be precisely targeted, not a flood of indiscriminate aid.
origin: Agricultural metaphor (drip irrigation vs flooding); government/policy jargon for targeted intervention

example_sentence: 她的朋友圈全是发疯文学。
example_english: Her social media is full of unhinged posts.
origin: Internet genre of deliberately over-the-top emotional writing; cathartic, absurdist, often humorous

meaning: background content to watch while eating/digital side dish
example_sentence: 吃饭的时候需要点电子榨菜。
example_english: I need some digital side-dish content to watch while eating.
origin: 榨菜 (pickled mustard) is a simple side dish; 电子榨菜 = easy-watching video content consumed during meals

example_sentence: 这个城市夜景太赛博朋克了。
example_english: This city's night view is so cyberpunk.
origin: Transliteration of 'cyberpunk'; used beyond sci-fi to describe any surreal, high-tech-meets-gritty-reality scene

pinyin: jì yào yòu yào hái yào
meaning: wanting everything at once/having it all (often critical)
example_sentence: 老板既要速度又要质量还要省钱。
example_english: The boss wants speed AND quality AND cost savings.
origin: Parody of political rhetoric structure; now used to mock impossible simultaneous demands
